statement.en: Probably Approximately Consensus: On the Learning Theory of Finding Common Ground
statement.summary: A primary goal of online deliberation platforms is to identify ideas that are broadly agreeable to a community of users through their expressed preferences. Yet, consensus elicitation should ideally extend beyond the specific statements provided by users and should incorporate the relative salience of particular topics. We address this issue by modelling consensus as an interval in a one-dimensional opinion space derived from potentially high-dimensional data via embedding and dimensionality reduction. We define an objective that maximizes expected agreement within a hypothesis interval where the expectation is over an underlying distribution of issues, implicitly taking into account their salience. We propose an efficient Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) algorithm and establish PAC-learn

proposer: Carter Blair, Ben Armstrong, Shiri Alouf-Heffetz et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The paper presents a novel theoretical framework (PAC-learning for consensus intervals) that is mathematically rigorous but remains unpublished/preprint status (2026-04 dating suggests future or draft). The core consensus-finding mechanism is theoretically sound but requires empirical validation on real deliberation platforms. The salience-aware objective is innovative but not yet established as canonical in consensus literature.
